utterances: But in at least one case, the strategy got ahead of the Congressman's voting record.

Democratic strategist Stephanie Cutter.
It's not about what ads that you run, it's about what policies that you stand for.

Tamara Keith, NPR News.
President Trump's state of emergency is facing several legal challenges already.
And among the first to file a lawsuit were landowners in Texas.
This week has been one of the worst weeks ever.
Nayda Alvarez owns a home near the Rio Grande river along the U.S.-Mexico border.
A few months ago, she got a letter from the government saying her property is in the path of President Trump's proposed wall.
The government could build a wall through her land if it got the funding.
I'm talking about, you know, my grandfather's land, my parents'.
And my house is at the very back in the corner.
It's about 200 feet away from the river.
It's very quiet, peaceful.
You know, you hear the birds singing.
And then President Trump declared a national emergency and got that funding.
I mean, I was in shock.
Part of what made it hard to talk about was that the bully was one of her friends.
Well, about 20 years ago.
When I asked an elderly colleague, why do sex exist?
And he said well, you know about the Bdelloid rotifers.
I'd never heard about them.
But that's when we began to look at them, because of this interest in the question of why it takes two, instead of just one to make an offspring?
Yeah.
Could you please explain to us how that is the case?
Why does two help you so much avoid extinction?
Ah.
Well, this is one of the major problems in evolutionary biology.
There are more that 20 hypothesis to explain it.
The answer is, there is no general agreement.
So, we thought that by studying these in the laboratory, maybe we would find out.
But it turns out that they, as Irina and Eugene discovered, they can pick up genes from their environment.
What we don't know is if they can exchange genes homologously, that is, plug a gene from another rotifer into the same place where that gene was in the parent rotifer.
That would be more or less equivalent to sex.
What we published about is not equivalent to sex, it's picking up foreign genes, and putting them in a very special place near the ends of the chromosomes.
That's not replacing genes in the same place where the corresponding gene used to be.
I see.
We're talking to Matthew Meselson from Harvard University.
And if you would like to get in on the conversation, give us a call.
Our number is 1-800-989-8255.
That's 1-800-989-TALK.
Talking about a small organism that might explain the evolution or something about sex.
Why sex happens sometimes?
And why apparently in these organisms it doesn't happen?
Now, do you have any idea how recently these organisms were - have stolen these genes?
Could this have been happening along time ago?
Or do you think they're sort of cruising through the environment, picking them up as they bump into them?
We have no accurate measure of how recently - the most recent ones have been picked up.
We think that the older ones have been there for quite a while, because they've acquired things called introns.
But the short answer is we don't know.
Hm.
And it's amazing to me that they were able to pick them up from all sort of other animals.
How could they get one from a plant for example?
When they're dried out, these animals, their DNA is all broken up and their membranes are ruptured, and so foreign DNA could enter.
The repair of broken DNA involves in some cases, the addition of bits of DNA patching between the two pieces or more pieces that are broken.
So that may be one way in which the foreign DNA gets in.
The next experiment that needs to be done is to find out if they can trade DNA, or get DNA from dead rotifers of the same species, and have those genes replace their own genes.
That would be equivalent to sex.
That would be like genetic recombination.
They are amazing in another respect.
They're enormously resistant to radiation, more than any other animal that has been studied.
And this, whatever it is that protects them against radiation, may have important implications for inflammation, for cancer, for aging, for certain neurodegenerative diseases.
All things that may be connected with what are called reactive oxygen species that are generated by our metabolism, but also by radiation.
Hm.
Do you think there might be some secrets in there that would help human beings more tolerant of radiation?
Yes.
Although, I don't think human beings need to be more tolerant of radiation.
But they do need perhaps in some cases to be more tolerant of the oxygen, reactive oxygen molecules that are made normally in our bodies by our energy-producing mitochondria.
I see.
Now, some people may recognize your name as somebody from some years ago, who was interested in biological weaponry and - or not in producing it, but in controlling it of course.
And you did - you're famous for recognizing that yellow rain in Vietnam was apparently just bee poop, not a biological attack for example.
That's right.
And is there any connection between that, and what you're studying now?
Or is it just a - your just following your curiosity through science?
No connection at all, just curiosity.
Very good.
Well, it's a delight to talk to you.
Thank you so much for joining us.
You're very welcome.
Matthew Meselson is a Thomas Dudley Cabot Professor of natural sciences at Harvard University.
And he joined us by phone from Paris.

Suddenly today, everything's exploded.

Special Deterrence Force.
Yes.

And solar-power projects are already being placed on hold, since Congress has so far failed to extend federal tax credits for the industry that expire at the end of the year.
Combined, these two factors have effectively stalled the solar industry in the U.S., just as it was beginning to scale up.
Another issue, is that there's been a supplied bottle-neck in the silicon wafers used to make these - the sol - those photovoltaic panels.
That's been keeping prices really high.
But as silicon production ramps up in the coming year, prices are expected to plummet.
And that could go a long way toward making solar power, more mainstream - a more mainstream energy choice.

But solar power advocates say unfortunately, other countries like Spain with stronger supports, may be a better poised to take advantage of that boom, as well as the thousands of jobs it would create.
Thanks, Sam.

From NPR News, this is DAY TO DAY.
